paragraphs.body: Butterfly In Silence: To Hug Them One Last Time

War is not just a political conflict between two armed groups. It’s not the maps, the headlines, or the number of casualties read off screens. It’s not only a clash of armies—it’s what lingers in the silence after the noise. War is the quiet chaos that invades your body, your heart, your memories. It is not the sound of bombs; it is the silence after, when you realize that everything you once knew is now lost, stolen, or broken.

annotations.text: War is the quiet chaos that invades your body, your heart, your memories. It is not the sound of bombs; it is the silence after, when you realize that everything you once knew is now lost, stolen, or broken.

paragraphs.body: It was another morning of hassle, just like the last few days. We were still in the safe house, protected and waiting for the flight to leave the country. Like every other day, I sat by the window, watching the outside. It was sunny—warmer than usual—and the staff were preparing breakfast for all the families. An old man with a long white beard was responsible for the tea. He burned wood to boil water, and the smoke rose slowly, filling the air with a thick, earthy scent. After breakfast, a little girl from another family came over—I'd braided her hair the day before, and she wanted me to do it again. We sat on the floor. My five-year-old niece was beside me, and the house echoed with the laughs and shouts of my other niece and nephew playing nearby.

annotations.text: An old man with a long white beard was responsible for the tea. He burned wood to boil water, and the smoke rose slowly, filling the air with a thick, earthy scent.
annotations.comment: these details are so lovely--so quiet and tender.

annotations.text: After breakfast, a little girl from another family came over—I'd braided her hair the day before, and she wanted me to do it again.

paragraphs.body: I braided one side of the girl’s hair, but then the noise outside grew loud. I went to the window and pulled back the curtain. There were multiple buses parked outside, and the yard had filled with people in what felt like seconds. My brother came in and said, “Get ready. We’re leaving today.” I was thrilled. I started dreaming of school again, of a new life—until he added, “Only four of us are going.” “Wait… but what about the rest of us? We’re a family.” “They’re on the next flight,” he said. “They’ll come after us.”

And I trusted him. I trusted the people in charge. I trusted the process. So much trust that I didn’t even say goodbye properly. I didn’t hug them tightly. I didn’t say all the things I should have said. I wish every single day that I had hugged them. I wish I could bring that day back— just to hold them tight, to say goodbye the way I should have, to freeze that moment before everything changed.

annotations.text: And I trusted him. I trusted the people in charge. I trusted the process. So

paragraphs.body: We flew to Abu Dhabi, and I was out—but my heart was still trapped there. The silence that followed was the worst kind. The room that once overflowed with the laughter of kids was now still. Once again, I sat by the window, watching other children play outside, praying for news that my sisters would come. For almost a year, I woke up with trust and hope in my heart. But every day, there was nothing. I had trusted fate too much. I had trusted people too much. And in the end, my sisters never came. My family never came back together. And all I can think of now is how much I wish I had hugged them one last time.

annotations.comment: out of _______________? Be clear, here, so your reader knows what part of the world we're in. :)

paragraphs.body: That’s what war does—it doesn’t just tear families apart; it tears apart the part of you that used to believe in connection.

annotations.text: That’s what war does—it doesn’t just tear families apart; it tears apart the part of you that used to believe in connection.

paragraphs.body: That night at the safe house taught me how quickly trust can break, how people can make promises and still leave you waiting forever. Now, I’m afraid of making new bonds, of letting people in. What if they leave too? What if I trust again, and it ends the same way? That’s what war does—it doesn’t just separate people physically; it leaves scars on how you love, connect, and open up. It fills the spaces where peace should be with fear, silence, and longing. This isn’t just a story about what happened. It’s about what was felt, what was lost, and what continues to be carried. It’s about war—but not the kind shown in headlines. The kind that hides inside people.

annotations.text: That’s what war does—it doesn’t just separate people physically; it leaves scars on how you love, connect, and open up. It fills the spaces where peace should be with fear, silence, and longing.

This isn’t just a story about what happened. It’s about what was felt, what was lost, and what continues to be carried. It’s about war—but not the kind shown in headlines. The kind that hides inside people.

paragraphs.body: I bought a bracelet from a cheap online store. I didn’t know why it pulled me in, but now I understand. There’s a butterfly inside one of its beads—beautiful, but frozen in place, suspended over tiny flower blossoms. When I look at it now, I realize that butterfly is me. Still. Quiet. Trapped in a memory I never got to finish. Maybe that’s why I still wear it—not just because it’s pretty, but because it reminds me of what I’ve carried silently, and what I’m still carrying.

annotations.text: When I look at it now, I realize that butterfly is me. Still. Quiet. Trapped in a memory I never got to finish. Maybe that’s why I still wear it—not just because it’s pretty, but because it reminds me of what I’ve carried silently, and what I’m still carrying.
annotations.comment: Fabulous move to reveal the metaphor. Especially when the metaphor transcends cliche and becomes so quietly powerful.

paragraphs.body: The bead is clear and round, likely made of resin or glass, and its transparency magnifies what’s inside—like zooming into a moment. The butterfly looks peaceful, resting on blossoms, but there’s also a sadness. It feels like it’s been captured in stillness, held in a moment it can never leave. Its wings are red, white, and dark blue, fading from top to bottom. It stands out against the soft cream color of the bracelet’s cord—almost like it was meant to be seen, to hold your gaze… and to haunt it. On either side of the butterfly are two small white beads, adding quiet balance. The nylon cord is adjustable, and depending on the day, I wear it tighter or looser—like I’m trying to control how much of my emotions I’m ready to feel.

annotations.comment: oh wow. The connotation of this word changes the tone of this paragraph in a powerful way.

paragraphs.body: That’s the thing about war—it doesn’t end when you reach safety. It lives on in what you carry.

annotations.text: That’s the thing about war—it doesn’t end when you reach safety. It lives on in what you carry.
annotations.comment: The refrains about war resonate throughout this essay and give the essay a tone and shape and voice that feels almost like a litany, like a prayer. It's beautiful.

paragraphs.body: War is not just a conflict of armies; it’s the quiet assassination of the future. War is the goodbye you didn’t know was the last. It’s the trust you place in people who say, “It’s going to be okay,” and the heartbreak when it’s not. War is sisters who stay behind, promises that never get fulfilled, and memories that ache louder with time. War is the face of a baby who never knew his father. It’s a mother mourning a child she never buried. It’s the quiet kind of pain—the one you carry without words, even in safe places.

War is the silence that follows. It is the reason I second-guess love, connection, and safety. It is the stillness in my chest when I laugh too hard, like I’m not allowed to. It is walking into a safe room and still looking for exits. War is not just something you survive—it is something you carry. It is the butterfly inside that bead—still, quiet, and trapped. War is not over when the bullets stop. It is what lives in the absence, in the silence, in the longing. It is the ache that stays long after the world believes you’re okay.

annotations.text: It is the stillness in my chest when I laugh too hard, like I’m not allowed to. It is walking into a safe room and still looking for exits. War is not just something you survive—it is something you carry.

paragraphs.body: The bracelet holds more than a butterfly—it holds a version of me I never got to finish. It represents what war left behind. The stillness. The sadness. The strength, maybe. And the hope that one day, something inside will finally be free.

War is Farkhunda. The ignorance that accused her, the fear that believed it, and the silence that let it happen. War is not just what burned her body—it’s what burned truth, justice, and humanity. War is the girls from Dasht-e-Barchi who never came back from school. War is the baby who never felt the warmth of their mother’s embrace, lost in the chaos of an attack at a hospital meant to bring life, not death. It is the mothers who never got to hear their child’s first cry, who never got to make a first memory.

annotations.text: War is Farkhunda. The ignorance that accused her, the fear that believed it, and the silence that let it happen. War is not just what burned her body—it’s what burned truth, justice, and humanity. War is the girls from Dasht-e-Barchi who never came back from school. War is the baby who never felt the warmth of their mother’s embrace, lost in the chaos of an attack at a hospital meant to bring life, not death. It is the mothers who never got to hear their child’s first cry, who never got to make a first memory.
annotations.comment: This is amazingly powerful. Wow. This is a humbling essay.

paragraphs.body: I want to forget these memories, but the pain is still there, and it doesn’t let me. It stays in the background, even when you try to move forward. I wish I was the only one, but millions of others carry this pain too—people who don’t always get the chance to say it out loud, who live with their sorrow in silence. People who’ve lived through war, loss, separation, and the deep unfairness of a world that often looks away. The world is both beautiful and cruel, sometimes in the same breath. It gives some people peace and comfort while giving others nothing but fear and fire. It lets one child dream of birthdays and bikes while another learns what airstrikes sound like. It’s like the butterfly in my bracelet: so beautiful, so delicate, but trapped in stillness—just like those who’ve survived the unlivable. Seen by some, but truly understood by so few.

annotations.text: The world is both beautiful and cruel, sometimes in the same breath.
annotations.comment: What a line. I know I keep saying "wow," but wow.

paragraphs.body: And so, I carry this butterfly inside me—still, quiet, and trapped. It reminds me daily of what I’ve lost and what so many others have lost in the aftermath of war. This butterfly is not a symbol of beauty, but of endurance—of surviving despite the silence, the absence, and the pain. War is not a distant event that only affects those who live through it. It is in the hearts of the survivors, carried in the quiet moments, in the awkward laughter, in the tears that no one sees. But we endure. We rebuild, even when it feels impossible. And the pain of it is something you can never skip—you just get used to it. And that is not okay. War. Separation. Fear. Silence. None of it should ever be part of childhood. Or family. Or growing up. But it is—for me and for so many others. Just remember, behind every refugee statistic, there’s a little girl braiding someone’s hair one last time. There’s a heartbreaking silence. There’s a butterfly, still and beautiful, trying to survive. Perhaps true peace is not just the absence of war, but the presence of the quiet, tender moments we often forget—the warmth of a hug, the safety of family, and the hope that one day, all of us will be free from the shadows of suffering.

annotations.comment: These one word sentences (you did this earlier in the essay, too) create a striking and rhetorically meaningful rhythm.
The repetition of the metaphor is also an amazing move.

It is truly humbling for not only its message but the poetry, the sophistication, the rhythms, the structure, the nuances, the metaphors, the voice, the depth, the beauty of the writing. I am not kidding when I say that this essay should be published int he world. Everyone should read it. I can’t wait to talk with you about it.
